compositional_truth_check.reconciliation_notes: The biome has suffered a near-total collapse of biological specimens. The P3 Pothos (anchor) is absent/withered, and the P2/P4 specimens are no longer visible as living tissue.

inventory_reconciliation.P3_Pothos: Systemic Loss (Anchor rabbit present, plant absent)

plant_audit.P1: No plant material detected. Soil appears dry and undisturbed.
plant_audit.P2_P4_Cluster: The black pot contains only debris (eggshell fragments). No viable plant tissue remains.
plant_audit.P3: The white rabbit anchor remains, but the Pothos foliage is gone.

biome_observations.soil_condition: Desiccated, dark, and devoid of organic growth.
biome_observations.debris: Persistent presence of eggshell fragments in the black pot; electronic component (IC chip/header) present on the desk surface.
biome_observations.fungal_presence: None detected; environment appears too dry for active fungal colonization.

temporal_deltas.earliest_to_t4: Initial state showed minimal green foliage in the black pot.
temporal_deltas.t4_to_t2: Progressive loss of leaf turgor and eventual disappearance of the remaining green stem.
temporal_deltas.t2_to_current: Total senescence. The desk surface now only contains inert objects (rabbit, eggshells, electronic component).

vision_report.visual_health_inference: The biome is currently non-viable. All registered plants have reached a state of total necrosis or have been removed, leaving only the substrate and non-biological artifacts.
anomalies: Introduction of an electronic component (IC header) on the desk surface.
Persistent use of eggshells as a soil amendment/top dressing, which has failed to prevent systemic collapse.
vision_report.narrative_description: The botanical audit reveals a catastrophic loss of all specimens. Over the 5-day observation period, the plants transitioned from a state of visible stress to complete disappearance. The current environment is an inert display of empty pots and non-biological debris. No active photosynthesis or growth is occurring.
